title: Advanced Dungeon Goons - Review and Session Recap (solo RPG)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BNze82M59-E

Daniel (@dungeondive) opens with an atmospheric session recap — Deacon Darius of the Abbey of the Final Star rowing to a necromancer-haunted island to recover a holy relic — then reviews the engine behind it: Advanced Dungeon Goons by Jacob Fleming (Gelatinous Cubism Games), a rules-light fantasy RPG built on Nate Treme's Tunnel Goons.

The system is elegantly simple: roll 2d6 plus a relevant stat against a difficulty score; the gap on a failed dangerous roll is the damage you take. Stats double as classes — Brute, Dexterity, Sage — and a clever combat wrinkle ties an enemy's difficulty score to its health, so foes get easier to hit as they weaken. He praises the art highly, while flagging genuinely murky combat rules (the dodge mechanic baffles him, and shields lack stated bonuses) — fixable by house rule.

Crucially it isn't built for solo, so he layers on tools: the Perplexing Ruins oracle, Table Fables 2, and two card decks — Philip Reed's outdoor encounters (shuffled into a card "island" he explores) and Axebane's Deck of Many Dungeons, which he uses Warhammer-Quest-style to seed meaningful objective rooms. He keeps a campaign journal for Darius's ongoing relic hunt.

A charming, breezy, easily-soloed little system once you bolt on an oracle.

----

For a rules-light system like this, do you prefer it bare so you can house-rule freely, or would you rather it nailed down combat tightly?
